data.title: China woman shocked to find male therapist at spa appointment, is mocked for being ‘ignorant’
data.description:
A woman in central China who was lying naked while awaiting a massage at a spa was shocked when a male therapist appeared, and she refused his services.
Following her refusal she was mocked by the manager of the spa for being “ignorant”.
The incident unfolded on December 12 at Yushiman Hi-tech Anti-ageing Centre in Changsha, Hunan province.
The customer, surnamed Huang, undressed and lay on her stomach on a bed in anticipation of a shoulder and neck massage, Fengmang News reported.

Huang said she heard a female manager come into her room to place a towel over her.
Then another person came in and Huang immediately realised it was a man.
“I was naked at that time. So I said I did not accept a man to serve me. The female manager asked me why I could not accept a male therapist. I said, why should I accept a male therapist when I was naked,” Huang was quoted as saying.
“The manager told me not to be so ignorant. She said there are also male doctors at hospitals and male therapists at foot massage parlours,” said Huang.
She threatened to call the police during the altercation.

The manager agreed to change a therapist, but Huang said she was no longer in the mood for a massage.
“What the manager said made me uncomfortable. She thought I was making a big deal out of it. She said I was ignorant and that the male worker was handsome and in his 20s,” said Huang.
“She told me I was old enough to be his mother and as a result he would not be interested in me,” she added.
Huang said that despite the fact that the male therapist did not touch her body, she still regarded it as an offence because he entered the room without her permission.
The manager said they sent a man to attend to Huang because she did not specify which gender she wanted.

Finally, the manager apologised to Huang.
“Initially I did not think I was wrong. In many spas, it is common practice that male therapists serve female customers, while females massage the males,” said the unidentified manager.
The story went viral on mainland social media.
“The spa was wrong. They should check with the customer first. Also, their attitude was unpleasant,” said one online observer.
But another person said: “My understanding is that massage centres send therapists to customers of the opposite gender. Otherwise, customers will get angry.”

data.title: What’s next for Katseye? The ‘global girl group’ on their Beautiful Chaos Tour and reclaiming their lives as 20-year-olds
data.description:
Almost every Katseye performance begins the same way. The bass notes of the girl group’s first single “Debut” echo through the venue, bright stage lights shine on its members and a screaming crowd nearly drowns out their vocals.
But roughly a week before heading out on their first official tour, their opening number was met with a few sparse claps.

Inside the Hollywood Palladium, the group – fresh-faced, high-spirited and decked out in their colour-coordinated stagewear – held their final dress rehearsal last month. It was both the first time they’d run the show on a stage and the last time they would practice before the sold-out Beautiful Chaos Tour.
Their final stop on the North American trek was in Los Angeles, where they played the YouTube Theater and the Palladium on December 12 and 13.
The global girl group, made up of Megan Skiendiel, Sophia Laforteza, Daniela Avanzini, Lara Raj, Yoonchae Jeung and Manon Bannerman, is in the midst of a rapid rise to fame. In a matter of months – since the release of their second EP, Beautiful Chaos, in June – they have continued to make each of their pop star dreams come true, with anything from the viral Gap campaign, a record-breaking crowd at Lollapalooza and two Grammy nominations.

The group originated from a YouTube reality competition show called The Debut: Dream Academy in 2023. Taking influence from the K-pop training system, over 120,000 contestants competed for a spot in what would become Katseye. The show was presented by Hybe, the South Korean entertainment company behind acts like BTS and Le Sserafim, and Geffen, a label of Universal Music, which shared the goal of crafting a multicultural girl group. (Avanzini is Venezuelan-Cuban; Raj is Indian; Skiendiel is Chinese Singaporean; Laforteza is Filipina; Bannerman is Swiss-Ghanaian; and Jeung is Korean.)

Their on-screen journey was simultaneously captured through the Netflix docuseries, Popstar Academy: Katseye, which took viewers inside their intense training and evaluations.
Two years after their debut, Katseye remains in a similar situation – where the stakes haven’t disappeared, but rather changed. They are no longer competing for a spot in the group; they are instead navigating their swift rise to stardom.
Fresh off the stage of the nearly empty venue, Katseye traded their Y2K-inspired performance looks for sweats and sat around a coffee table, indulging in late-night snacks. In between fits of laughter and finishing each other’s sentences, the members explain that they’d recently come back from a European press tour and only had a week to nail down their tour performance.
It sounds like you have been operating on crunch time for these upcoming shows. Do you usually have more time to prepare?

Raj: With every performance that we’ve ever done, we’ve always had a really extensive amount of rehearsal – sometimes a little too much rehearsal even. It takes a lot of time to make sure things are so synchronised and super sharp. This was our first time putting something together so fast.
Laforteza: Because of our growth and how fast everything is happening, we are still learning to become more seasoned and prepared in short amounts of time. It’s what all our previous training is for. Now we’re starting to figure out how to do a lot more things at once. We’re almost everywhere now. We’re all over the internet. We’re getting these nominations. We’re showing up to all these events, so we have a lot less time to prepare for certain things.
Katseye is in the middle of its biggest year so far. What’s it like being on the inside of that whirlwind?

Skiendiel: I’m still trying to grasp onto the feeling of being in Katseye. We got nominated for two Grammys a couple of days ago, and that was so exciting, but I was so focused on these tour rehearsals that I didn’t even really get to process it fully. You know, what’s actually crazy – yesterday we had an off day, and I was watching one of our older interviews. We said we wanted a VMA, a Grammy, to play Coachella and do a tour. Since then, we’ve won a VMA, received Grammy nominations, will play Coachella next year and are about to leave on tour.
It seems like you all have some kind of manifesting power. How does it feel to have chosen this pop star life and have it progress so quickly?

Avanzini: When we were all trainees, all of our goals were the same. Over these past three years, we see how much we’ve pushed ourselves and how resilient we can be. Now that we are currently accomplishing the goals we set out to do, it makes us want to think of new goals to accomplish.
Sometimes, though, we get so lost. All we see in our daily lives is travelling, rehearsals and shows. We get so caught up in our schedules.
Raj: We’ll often forget to reflect on and enjoy what’s happening. The only time I’m aware of what’s really happening is when we’re performing. There’s this moment of being on stage, and being aware of every single person.
What happens when this mindfulness takes over?

Avanzini: For me, I’m always so stressed out before [a show]. When I first started performing, I would just black out and do the performance. Then I’d get offstage and have no idea what happened.
But now we’re more comfortable on stage. It feels like home, especially because we’re with each other. It’s not as scary as being a soloist. It’s nice to be able to look around on stage and give each other a smile. It grounds me a lot.
When you were on stage performing in an empty venue, just a few minutes ago, it still seemed like you were having the time of your lives. What keeps you going, even after practising all day?

Laforteza: Passion!
[Each member audibly agrees between bites of Reese’s Peanut Butter Cups and Doritos.]
Raj: I f****** love giving a show. There’s nothing more that I love than being in a hot outfit with a fan blowing my hair and dancing. It’s like living in a fantasy. There is nothing more thrilling than that. That’s why we’ll stay here till 3am. We’ve been waiting for this [moment] since we were babies. This show is the entire reason we do it.
Jeung: When I was a K-pop trainee and was having a hard time, I would think of being on the stage with other girls and seeing all the fans. I would draw that picture in my head and want to stay there.
People have been fans of you before you were officially Katseye, back when you were reality TV contestants. You have grown so much since then, welcoming in new aesthetics, hairstyles and music production. What is your relationship to change like?

Skiendiel: The more we grow, the more stories we have to tell. Not all of our music is going to be like “Touch” or “Gnarly”. We’re now having so many new experiences that we’ve gathered from this era that we’re gonna put into our next EP.
Raj: Our personal lives were completely on the back burner for three years before this year. It was completely nonexistent. We didn’t go to parties and we didn’t see anyone. This year, I think we were able to prioritise having life experiences and being 19- and 20-year-olds. Our art is going to be so mundane and robotic if we’re not real people who are going out and trying new things.

Whatever we do next is going to have a lot more soul and offer authentic emotions and experiences. The art we make next will feel more like art rather than just a performance. It’s where we are now as a group. We’ve grown up a bit, and there will be more depth to what we have to say.

data.title: Netflix K-drama Idol I: Choi Soo-young, Kim Jae-young lead K-pop-infused legal drama
data.description:
Lead cast: Choi Soo-young, Kim Jae-young
Latest Nielsen rating: 2.26 per cent
As 2025 draws to a close, viewers are invited back into a Korean courtroom one last time with Idol I (also styled as I Dol I). This time you can leave your lawbooks behind, since the emphasis is squarely on character melodrama rather than legal procedure.
Choi Soo-young (Second Shot at Love) and Kim Jae-young (The Judge from Hell) team up as a lawyer and a K-pop star, respectively.


Choi – herself a member of the K-pop supergroup Girls’ Generation – plays Maeng Se-na, a young partner at her firm known for winning unwinnable cases that others refuse to touch.
Her latest challenge involves Do Ra-ik (Kim), a solo idol accused of murdering his friend and former bandmate, Kang Woo-sung (Ahn Woo-yeon, King the Land).
Se-na is accustomed to high-profile cases and immune to the media scrutiny that accompanies them, no matter how viciously she is criticised. However, this specific trial threatens to crash her professional life into her carefully guarded private world.
Off the clock, Se-na is secretly a devoted K-pop fan whose ultimate idol is none other than Ra-ik himself.

The series dedicates significant screen time to Se-na’s fixation, while simultaneously contrasting it with Ra-ik’s reality: a star struggling with the immense pressure of a solo career, weighed down by the expectations of fans, management and himself alike.
While Se-na the fangirl is fully realised, Se-na the lawyer or even Se-na the person remains a sketch.
After winning a major but vaguely defined case in the opening, she ignores her colleagues’ celebratory gestures upon her return to the office, skipping a victory party in her honour to rush home and obsess over Ra-ik.
Flashbacks reveal that Se-na’s devotion is rooted in trauma. As a bullied teenager contemplating suicide by a riverside, she was saved by the sound of Ra-ik busking nearby.

Her fandom was born from that moment of salvation, though he appears to have no memory of the encounter.
Despite hints at these dramatic underpinnings, the initial episodes largely portray Se-na as a hyper-competent but emotionally vacant automaton. The how of her success is clear, but the why of her personality remains missing.
The series demands viewers take Se-na’s prowess on faith. Her antisocial behaviour is treated as comedy, punctuated by aggressive sound effects, while her legal genius is merely asserted by other characters rather than demonstrated in court.
Momentum builds in the second episode as Se-na agrees to take Ra-ik’s case and the trial begins. She faces off against Kwak Byoung-kyung (Jeong Jae-kwang, A Hundred Memories), an arrogant prosecutor from an elite family.

He turns out to be yet another figure from Se-na’s past who does not recognise her: her school bully.
Also briefly introduced is Choi Hee-jin (Heavenly Ever After) as the mysterious Hong Hye-joo, who repeatedly crosses Ra-ik’s path, catching his attention at various events.
Although framed as a legal series, Idol I shows no ambition to be the next Extraordinary Attorney Woo. Its focus lies in character drama, though the execution remains lopsided for the moment.
Ra-ik has the contours of a character, albeit a thin one, while Se-na feels like a collection of tics and triggers. Despite Choi’s spirited performance, the writing has yet to lift the character off the page.

One hopes that as the story evolves, the leads will elevate each other – and that the procedural elements will rise to meet the potential of the premise.
Idol I is streaming on Netflix.

data.title: Supermaxis Comanche, LawConnect continue duel in Sydney to Hobart race, Hong Kong boat 3rd
data.description:
Supermaxis Master Lock Comanche and defending champion LawConnect continued their close duel on Saturday on the second day of the 80th running of the Sydney to Hobart yacht race.
Just over 27 hours into the race that began on Friday in Sydney Harbour and ends on the island state of Tasmania, Comanche had a four-nautical-mile lead over LawConnect.
LawConnect was forced to contend with a broken mainsheet and halyard overnight but managed to fix both issues and remain in pursuit of Comanche, which is skippered by Matt Allen and James Mayo.
“We had a few things slow us down and Comanche’s obviously done quite well,” LawConnect skipper Christian Beck said on Saturday. “It’s mildly disappointing for us, but you win some, lose some. Everything’s good, we’re still in the hunt.”
With more than half the race completed, Hong Kong yacht SHK Scallywag 100 was third, followed closely by the 88-foot Lucky, a New York Yacht Club entry skippered by Bryon Ehrhart.

The leading yachts were in Bass Strait, which separates the mainland from Tasmania. They were likely to finish at Constitution Dock in Hobart late on Sunday afternoon under current weather and wind projections – more than two days after the start and well outside the race record.
The race lost its first supermaxi when Wild Thing 100 retired. All crew members were safe as Wild Thing began the journey back to Sydney after reporting rigging damage.
Skipper Robert Large aboard his yacht Inukshuk was also among the 11 retirements after becoming entangled in a fishing net.
“We couldn’t move. It took us probably 45 minutes to clear the thing,” Large said. After finally pulling the net off, Large was concerned his boat may have sustained rudder damage, which would have made it risky to continue sailing.
An Australian resident for almost 20 years, Canadian-born Large has sailed in six previous Sydney-Hobart races.
LawConnect led the fleet out of Sydney harbour in search of its third straight line honours win. Overnight conditions on Friday night were rough on the fleet, but not as dangerous as last year when two sailors died in storms on the first night.
On Friday, the fleet paid tribute to the victims of the December 14 terror attack by scattering rose petals off the coast of Bondi Beach, off Sydney, as they passed the area early in the race.
LawConnect, owned by Australian tech millionaire Beck, won last year’s event in one day, 13 hours, 35 minutes and 13 seconds for the 628-nautical-mile (722-mile, 1,160km) race.
The race record set by LDV Comanche – one day, nine hours, 15 minutes and 24 seconds – has stood since 2017 and only comes under threat in very strong downwind conditions.

data.title: Indonesian rescuers search for Spanish family of 4 after tourist boat sinks
data.description:
A Spanish family of four is missing after a tourist boat sank in rough seas in Indonesia, state news agency Antara reported on Saturday.
The vessel was carrying 11 people when it sank in the Padar Island Strait near Labuan Bajo on Friday night, Antara said. The popular tourist destination is the main gateway to Komodo National Park, a Unesco World Heritage site.
Seven people, including two Spanish tourists, four crew members and a tour guide, were rescued.
A search for the remaining four was continuing on Saturday, with Padar Island closed to tourists due to extreme weather.
Those missing have been identified as Martin Carreras Fernando, Martin Garcia Mateo, Martines Ortuno Maria Lia and Martinez Ortuno Enriquejavier, according to the Jakarta Globe.
“The four victims are foreign tourists, Spanish citizens, who based on initial data were travelling as one family group,” the newspaper reported Labuan Bajo port authority chief Stephanus Risdiyanto as saying on Saturday.

According to authorities, the KM Putri Sakinah left Komodo Island for Padar Island at around 8pm on Friday for a sightseeing trip. About 30 minutes later, the traditional passenger boat reportedly suffered engine failure and sank.
The Labuan Bajo port authority attributed the sinking to high waves of up to 3 metres (10 feet). “This made it difficult for us to carry out the initial search,” Stephanus told Antara.
Video released by the National Search and Rescue Agency showed emergency workers in blue uniforms helping people, some barefoot, out of an inflatable orange boat and onto shore.
Other footage showed people in life jackets sitting on a rescue boat, and a similar vessel kicking up spray as it motored across the waves in the dark.
“Search operations are still ongoing, with priority given to the safety of [rescue] personnel and careful consideration of weather and sea conditions,” Stephanus said, according to the Jakarta Globe.
The island’s temporary closure was prompted by the accident and weather monitoring reports, he said.
“Last night, we issued a notice to mariners temporarily closing cruises to Komodo and Padar,” he told Antara, adding that the sailing permit service for the Padar-Komodo route would remain suspended until conditions improved.
Marine accidents occur regularly in Indonesia, a Southeast Asian archipelago of around 17,000 islands, often due to lax safety standards and bad weather.
Additional reporting by SCMP’s Asia desk

data.title: Hong Kong man arrested after extendable baton found in US-bound luggage
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested a man after finding an extendable baton in his checked luggage bound for the United States, marking the second discovery of unauthorised weapons in airline baggage this week.
The force received a report on Friday after airport security staff discovered the suspected unauthorised weapon in a checked suitcase and arrested the 62-year-old local man.
“Preliminary investigations showed that the man had hidden a suspected extendable baton, an unauthorised weapon, in his checked suitcase,” a force spokesman said.
The man has been released on bail while a crime squad from the Airport Police District continues its investigation.
The force warned that while some passengers might take extendable batons or pepper spray overseas, these items were classified as unauthorised weapons and could not be brought onto flights.
On Wednesday, a 45-year-old local woman was arrested for the same offence after airport security staff found a suspected extendable baton in her suitcase.
Her suitcase was checked luggage destined for Canada.
Carrying unauthorised weapons in checked or carry-on luggage is illegal and carries a maximum penalty of five years’ imprisonment.

data.title: Can AI deliver the economic goods and high returns? Don’t count on it
data.description:
Only one thing matches the excitement, bordering on hysteria, about artificial intelligence having the potential to transform just about every aspect of our lives – and that is the exuberance AI has generated in stock markets. Yet both phenomena are coming under increasing scrutiny as we enter the new year.
Some question the extent to which AI can in fact substitute for human intelligence across business and society while others challenge the technology on environmental and ethical grounds. Yet others fear the massive capital demands for investments in AI could precipitate a crisis in a banking and financial system already burdened by debt and asset bubbles.
These issues will need to be faced squarely in 2026 so that the headlong dash towards the brave new world of AI does not become a rout as the public rebels against its environmental costs and its impact on employment and social equity, while investors conclude that the returns do not match the colossal investments needed for the data centres that are integral to the AI revolution.
Adoption of AI in the Asia-Pacific is widespread and the advent of the technology could boost the regional economy, Asian Development Bank Institute (ADBI) dean Bambang Brodjonegoro noted during a recent conference in Tokyo. However, he also warned that major headwinds ranging from job losses and increased income inequality to the digital divide would need to be addressed.
The Tokyo forum, organised by the ADBI and the World Bank Group, also provided some revealing perspectives from World Bank chief economist Indermit Gill, Asian Development Bank chief economist Albert Park and Tshilidzi Marwala, rector of the United Nations University Institute for the Advanced Study of Sustainability.
On paper at least, the Asia-Pacific is well placed to take advantage of AI-enabled transformation and tech-driven industrial development. The region has a broad skills base and produces three-quarters of the world’s STEM graduates. On the other hand, a quarter of the region remains unconnected to the internet.

Artificial intelligence could thus represent a double-edged sword for development in Asia, carrying both promise and risk, Brodjonegoro said. On the one hand, it has the potential to accelerate innovation and growth. On the other, AI-driven automation is reshaping labour markets and exposing workers to new vulnerabilities.
This novel technology, he suggested, could expand the coverage and quality of essential social programmes amid mounting debt distress, demographic shifts, socioeconomic disparities and extreme weather events. AI could also enhance resource efficiency from crop yields to energy grids – although, if left unchecked, its expansion poses significant climate and water-related risks.
Overall, AI is predicted to lift annual GDP growth in the Asia-Pacific region by around 2 percentage points and raise regional productivity by up to 5 per cent. It could also generate around US$1 trillion in economic gains over the next decade for Southeast Asia alone.
While that is good news, such potential gains are far from evenly distributed. Early research suggests that a quarter of firms are facing job losses, with women, youth and low-skills sectors particularly vulnerable. The digital divide compounds risks. For example, while 40 per cent of jobs in Singapore are rated as complementary to AI, the share is just 3 per cent in Laos.
The potential downside does not stop there. While six countries in the Asia-Pacific region were responsible for 85 per cent of the gains from the digital economy in 2022, the least-developed countries together accounted for less than 1 per cent.


Data protection and cybersecurity challenges are rising too, with targeted deep fakes, scams and AI-enabled cyberattacks. Worse still, AI’s resource intensity poses deep sustainability concerns: its water use may soon exceed Denmark’s and its electricity demand is expected to surpass Japan’s, in a region where more than 80 per cent of energy comes from fossil fuels.
As Nikkei Asia noted in a recent report, Asia’s AI computing cluster landscape is changing, as emerging AI data centres give the region’s main data centre hubs, Japan and Singapore, a run for their money. Malaysia’s Johor and Indonesia’s Batam are prime examples of the new competition to host these computing clusters, while countries such as India and Thailand are seeking data centre investments.
Meanwhile, financial markets are becoming increasingly worried. As Jefferies equity strategy head Christopher Wood observed, US investors will be asking hard questions about the returns, or lack of them, from the AI capex arms race by the middle of next year.
“It looks like that process has already begun at the margin with growing focus on where companies are borrowing to finance the capex rather than spending from cash reserves,” Wood suggested. “The more the returns on AI capex are questioned, the more there will also be related concerns about the looming excess capacity in data centres. This in turn will raise concerns regarding those funding such data centre construction and related infrastructure.”
Soberingly too, Reuters noted in a recent report that “since ChatGPT exploded three years ago, companies big and small have leapt at the chance to adopt generative artificial intelligence and to stuff it into as many products as possible. But so far, the vast majority of businesses are struggling to realise a meaningful return on their AI investments.”
The most intelligent approach to embracing the AI revolution, it seems, is doing so cautiously and using native intelligence (or common sense) rather than being blinded by science.

data.title: Honouring ancestors, shaping empires: the story of Southeast Asia’s Peranakan Chinese
data.description:
In dark blue suits, the elders of the Khoo clan gathered outside the prayer halls of the Leong San Tong Khoo Kongsi, a grand 190-year-old clanhouse in George Town, the capital of the Malaysian state of Penang.
One by one, they stepped inside for the Tung Chen ceremony, held annually on the first day of the winter solstice, a ritual that draws together members of the Khoo clan, one of the oldest Peranakan communities, from far beyond Penang.
As smoke filled the air, Khoo Kay Hock, a senior trustee, filed into the hall clutching joss sticks. A short prayer and three bows later, he left, his respects paid to the ancestors and deities that have bound Peranakan families across Southeast Asia.
“We were here even before the British,” he told This Week in Asia, referring to the British arrival in Penang in 1786. His seafaring Khoo clansmen are believed to have established roots on the island as early as 1752.

The Tung Chen rites, centred on wooden ancestral tablets inscribed with the names of deceased forebears, have been practised for generations. But the ceremony also gestures towards a much wider cultural narrative.
That story is of a community that originated in China, took shape through migration and intermarriage across Southeast Asia’s port cities, and became one of the region’s most influential and far-reaching Chinese diasporas.
Shared customs, clothing and rituals only hint at the complexity of a group that travelled, traded and married across cultures, diversifying to include ethnic Indians and Arabs, while forging a distinct identity with its own Baba Malay language and dress, including the kebaya.
Today, Peranakans are found from Myanmar and Vietnam to Brunei and Singapore, their influence extending well beyond any single clan or city.
The Khoo clan, a prominent Hokkien lineage descended from migrants from Fujian in China, is best known for its ornate clanhouse complex in George Town, celebrated for its Chinese craftsmanship and long-standing role in community life.
“For me, being Peranakan is a cultural sensibility rather than a label,” said Salma Khoo Nasution, a historian and sixth-generation Peranakan from Penang.

Who are the Peranakans?
From as early as the 15th century, port cities such as Malacca (the old name for modern-day Melaka) and Batavia (which became Indonesia’s capital city of Jakarta) became meeting points where Chinese traders and local communities interacted, blurring cultural boundaries and laying the groundwork for the hybrid Peranakan identities that later spread across Southeast Asia.
These communities emerged well before the colonial era. European traders initially arrived for business, but colonial powers later asserted control by force as ambitions spread across Southeast Asia in the late 18th and 19th centuries, using maritime access to dominate trade routes and extract resources.
By the time Western powers claimed these ports, Peranakan communities were already established, shaped by generations of trade, settlement and intermarriage.
Malay became the shared language of commerce, culture and everyday communication – even the word Peranakan means “locally born” in Malay.
“This context is crucial to understanding how the Peranakan communities emerged,” said Peter Lee, founding curator of the National University of Singapore’s Baba House, a restored Peranakan home and heritage museum.

Peranakans with roots in Malacca began moving to Singapore soon after the British founded the port in 1819, bringing with them an urban culture shaped by centuries of exchange.
“Ports rose and fell constantly,” Lee said. “Local rulers tried to establish free ports, rivals destroyed them, alliances shifted and colonial powers intervened. The Peranakans found a ‘sweet spot’ within this system, but they were never isolated from it.”
Chinese men married local women who were not only Malay but also Siamese, Burmese, Javanese and others.
“These unions formed the core of the Peranakan community,” Salma said, adding that as more Chinese women migrated to the region in the early 20th century, Peranakans increasingly married among themselves.
The men were often deeply involved in clan associations and religious institutions, she said, while women were relatively more educated, valued and independent compared to many other communities at the time.
“Peranakan women enjoyed greater freedom and status, which is often overlooked,” Salma said.
As the community bloomed, its influence grew.
In Malaya, the British-ruled precursor to modern-day Malaysia, Tan Cheng Lock, a Peranakan rubber tycoon and politician from Malacca, devoted his life to fighting for the rights and social welfare of the Chinese community and played a major role in negotiations leading to independence in 1957.
In Singapore, doctor, social reformer and entrepreneur Lim Boon Keng co-founded the Oversea-Chinese Banking Corporation (OCBC) and championed the Malayan rubber industry.
Their social and economic prominence, however, also carried risks. During the Japanese occupation of Malaya and Singapore from 1942 to 1945, Peranakans were targeted for their perceived loyalty to the British.
Chinese communities, including Peranakans, faced executions and had their assets seized. Many wealthy families were forced to liquidate holdings, destroying fortunes built over generations.

A diaspora on the move
While Malacca and Singapore anchored the Peranakan presence in the southern Malay Peninsula, Penang and Phuket formed a northern axis, linked by geography and trade across the Andaman Sea and stretching as far north as Yangon.
These links were reinforced through long-standing ties across the Malay Peninsula.
“Penang families often sought brides from Malacca and Peranakan families from Phuket and Yangon later sought brides from Penang,” Lee said. “The network was extensive.”
In Singapore, Peranakans settled around Telok Ayer and nearby streets soon after the island was founded as a free port, forming a community at the heart of the colonial city.
Today, Peranakan scholars and cultural advocates are increasingly active in efforts to preserve and revive the community’s heritage. Figures such as Lee have been central to initiatives including the successful push to have the kebaya recognised on Unesco’s Intangible Cultural Heritage list, together with Malaysia, Brunei, Indonesia and Thailand.
In the northern region, Penang served as a key entrepot, re-exporting tin mined in Phuket and supplying provisions to the island, while also acting as a cultural reference point for Chinese entrepreneurs.
Salma said the Phuket Peranakan community emerged through Penang-linked networks, sending their children back by sea to be educated in Peranakan traditions – rooted in Chinese communities that grew up along the Strait of Malacca – rather than to Bangkok.
Over time, the diaspora expanded and diversified through intermarriage. Not everyone who identifies as Peranakan today descends from the same background.

“Many families are mixed – half, quarter, or even one-eighth Peranakan,” Salma said. “This is why cultural expression today often focuses on visible aspects like food and dress rather than deeper social structures.”
Peranakan identity, she added, is grounded in food, values, language and social behaviour.
From the kebaya – an intricately embroidered blouse worn with a batik skirt – to its cuisine, Peranakan culture reflects the blend of influences from which it emerged. Language remains central, with Hokkien widely used among older generations in places such as Penang.
The language, Salma said, carries values, world view and social boundaries shaped over centuries.
But the community faces the pressures of modern life. More families are English-speaking and send their children overseas for education, leading to a significant brain drain.
While interest among younger generations in reconnecting through food and tradition is growing, language transmission – especially Hokkien – has weakened.
“Reviving culture depends heavily on intergenerational transmission,” Salma said. “When families no longer live together or insist on passing it down, much of the depth is lost.”

data.title: China influencer with 44 million followers banned for zooming in on women’s breasts in live-stream
data.description:
A Chinese influencer with 44 million online followers has been banned for live-streaming a “vulgar” pool party at a hot spring.
The influencer, who uses the handle Liu Ergou, live-streamed the party involving about 20 people for nearly three hours.
It reportedly attracted more than a million viewers on a short video platform where Liu had 44 million followers.

The live-stream features many close-ups of scantily clad women’s breasts and hips.
Liu and other male participants are also seen playing with the women and throwing them into the pool.
Liu also applies body lotion to one woman to sell the product.
Some viewers are understood to have reported the event to the platform for having vulgar content.

However, clips of the live-stream continued to circulate.
More than 10 days later, the short video platform told the mainland media outlet Jimu News that it had banned Liu’s account for “violating rules”.
Some people said they were shocked by the platform’s slow reaction and suspected that it indulges such content to attract traffic.
Liu, from southwestern China’s Sichuan province, is infamous for featuring vulgarity in his videos. He reportedly swore and bragged about his history of gambling during live-streams.

He also incited other live-streamers to fight each other and take part in extreme challenges, such as eating spicy oil, to grab attention.
People also thought the platform’s punishment was too light as it only banned him from posting new content. His account was still visible and accepting new followers.
Also, a live-streamer in Liu’s company comforted his fans by saying that he was selecting products to sell in future events, appearing to be unaffected by the ban.
“The platform should totally block his account,” one online observer said.
“The mild punishment suggests that the platform is protecting him. The platform benefits from such traps just as much as the influencer himself,” said another.

“Liu has such a big fan base and is obviously a bad influence on young people. He should be fully banned. The authorities should supervise the platform,” said a third.
The platform involved is one of the most used on the mainland.
Qinglang, or “clean and bright”, campaigns were launched by China’s cyberspace administration in 2016 to tackle vulgar content.
The administration holds the platforms jointly liable for failing to handle violations.
The platform involved in the case of Liu was reportedly punished by Beijing’s law enforcers in 2018 and 2019, partly for vulgar content.

data.title: A year after near total blackout, China builds world’s largest smart transformer
data.description:
Just over a year after an incident that nearly triggered a cascading failure across China’s national power grid, engineers have developed the most powerful smart direct current (DC) transformer on the planet – an innovation poised to neutralise renewable energy shocks to the system.
The technology addresses an urgent need following a near total blackout in August last year, when low-frequency swings triggered by poorly integrated wind energy in the Xinjiang Uygur autonomous region threatened to destabilise the national grid.
Changzhou Xidian Transformer, based in the eastern province of Jiangsu, said the 750 million volt-ampere transformer had “set the world record for the highest capacity of a single converter transformer in the field of flexible direct current transmission”, according to a report from Chinese news outlet Jinbaonet earlier this month.
“[The technology] can effectively address the instability of high-proportion renewable energy generation at the sending end,” the news outlet said, adding that it greatly improved the safety, stability and operational flexibility of a large power grid.
The equipment was developed to support electricity transmission from the northwestern province of Gansu to eastern Zhejiang province as part of the national west-to-east electricity transmission project, according to Jinbaonet. It is the world’s first ultra-high voltage flexible direct current transmission project.
Measuring 2,370km (1,473 miles), the system will be able to send more than 36 billion kilowatt-hours (kWh) of clean electricity to Zhejiang per year after commissioning.
China channels electricity from its less-developed western regions – where the clean energy sector is booming – to the power-hungry eastern provinces.


For electricity to travel long distances, high-voltage direct current (HVDC) transmission has emerged as an economical solution with low transmission losses, improved grid stability and greater flexibility in integrating renewable energy sources, addressing the limitations of conventional alternating current (AC) transmission systems.
Renewable energy sources, such as wind and solar, rely on the weather and are unstable. Their intermittent nature poses challenges to grid stability and reliability.
To improve long-distance electric power transmission in modern power systems, Changzhou Xidian Transformer – under state-owned China XD Group – developed the new large-capacity flexible DC transformer.
It transforms AC – electric current that reverses direction periodically – to DC, which flows steadily in one direction.
The researchers said they had implemented advanced techniques to control magnetic flux leakage and monitor temperatures using fibre optics to ensure the reliability and long-term stability of the system under extreme operating conditions.
Their tests, including those for lightning strikes and switching between AC and DC, show that the system is reliable, with measurements of temperature rise and energy loss exceeding the design specifications, according to the team.
In 2018, German technology conglomerate Siemens announced that it had delivered the most powerful HVDC transformer at the time to China.
It was the world’s first 1,100-kilovolt transformer, which enabled transmission with low losses over almost 3,300km with a transmission capacity of 12 gigawatts between Xinjiang in the west and Anhui province in the east.
“HVDC transformers are part of the converter station that convert alternating current into direct current at the beginning of the transmission line and convert it back again at the end of the line. The conversion from alternating current to direct current helps to send energy over long distances with low losses,” the German company said.
